```python
# test_reverse_words.py
import pytest

# Adjust this import to match where reverse_words is defined.
# Example: from my_module import reverse_words
from your_module import reverse_words


@pytest.mark.parametrize(
    ("input_str", "expected"),
    [
        ("hello world", "world hello"),
        ("the quick brown fox", "fox brown quick the"),
        ("hello", "hello"),
        ("Hello, world!", "world! Hello,"),
        ("café au lait", "lait au café"),
    ],
)
def test_reverse_words_happy_path(input_str, expected):
    assert reverse_words(input_str) == expected


@pytest.mark.parametrize(
    ("input_str", "expected"),
    [
        ("", ""),
        ("   ", ""),
        ("  hello world  ", "world hello"),
        ("hello   world", "world hello"),
        ("hello\tworld", "world hello"),
        ("hello\nworld", "world hello"),
        ("  a\tb\nc  ", "c b a"),
        ("\thello\nworld\r\nagain ", "again world hello"),
    ],
)
def test_reverse_words_whitespace_edge_cases(input_str, expected):
    assert reverse_words(input_str) == expected


@pytest.mark.parametrize(
    ("bad_input", "expected_exception"),
    [
        (None, AttributeError),
        (123, AttributeError),
        (["hello", "world"], AttributeError),
        (b"hello world", TypeError),
    ],
)
def test_reverse_words_invalid_input_types_raise(bad_input, expected_exception):
    with pytest.raises(expected_exception):
        reverse_words(bad_input)
```
